verses.flow: Then the men of Judah came and there they anointed David king over the house of Judah. And they told David, saying, "It was the men of Jabesh-gilead who buried Saul."
verses.note: CHRIST TYPOLOGY: David's anointing as king over Judah is the second of three anointings: (1) privately by Samuel (1 Sam. 16:13), (2) over Judah at Hebron (here), (3) over all Israel (2 Sam. 5:3). Christ similarly has a private anointing (baptism, Matt. 3:16-17), a partial recognition (by His disciples), and a final universal enthronement (Phil. 2:9-11). David reigns first over his own tribe before all Israel; Christ first gathers His remnant before ruling all nations.

verses.flow: And when they came to the threshing floor of Nacon, Uzzah reached out toward the ark of God and took hold of it, for the oxen nearly upset it.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Uzzah's death at the threshing floor of Nacon (also called Chidon in 1 Chr. 13:9) is a watershed moment for understanding holiness. The ark was to be carried by Levites using poles (Num. 4:15; 7:9). Using a cart imitated the Philistines (1 Sam. 6:7). Uzzah's well-intentioned touch violated the explicit command: "they shall not touch the holy things, lest they die" (Num. 4:15). Good intentions do not override God's prescribed means of approach. Holiness is not a metaphor; it is a consuming reality.

verses.note: HEBREW: Three key words define the covenant: (1) bayit (house/dynasty) -- David's lineage will never be permanently cut off; (2) mamlakhah (kingdom) -- David's dominion is secured by God, not human power; (3) kisse' (throne) -- the seat of royal authority will endure 'ad 'olam (forever). These three realities -- dynasty, dominion, and throne -- converge in Christ. Rev. 22:16: "I am the Root and the Offspring of David." He is both David's source (Root) and David's heir (Offspring). verses.note: CHRIST TYPOLOGY: "The kindness of God" (chesed 'elohim) -- David's kindness to Mephibosheth is explicitly called God's own kindness channeled through the king. This is a profound type of grace. Mephibosheth is: (1) from the house of the king's enemy, (2) crippled and helpless, (3) living in Lo-debar ("no pasture/no word" -- a place of desolation), (4) unable to come to the king on his own, (5) brought to the king's table to eat continually. This is the gospel pattern: we were enemies (Rom. 5:10), helpless (Rom. 5:6), in a barren place, unable to approach God, yet brought to His table by sheer grace. Mephibosheth contributes nothing; he receives everything.

verses.flow: Then David said to Nathan, "I have sinned against the LORD." And Nathan said to David, "The LORD also has taken away your sin; you shall not die.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "I have sinned against the LORD" (chata'ti la'YHWH) -- David's confession is two Hebrew words: chata'ti laYHWH. No excuses, no blame-shifting, no qualifications. Compare Saul's confession (1 Sam. 15:24-25) which immediately seeks to save face. David's full repentance is Psalm 51, composed in response to this moment. Nathan's response is equally terse: "The LORD also has taken away your sin" (gam YHWH he'evir chatat'kha). The verb he'evir means "caused to pass over, removed." Forgiveness is instantaneous upon genuine repentance, but consequences remain.

verses.flow: However, because by this deed you have given great occasion to the enemies of the LORD to blaspheme, the child also that is born to you shall surely die."
verses.note: HEBREW: "Given great occasion to the enemies of the LORD to blaspheme" (ni'ets ni'atsta 'et 'oyvey YHWH) -- the infinitive absolute construction (ni'ets ni'atsta) intensifies the accusation: "you have utterly caused contempt." David's sin provides ammunition for God's enemies. The leader's failure has public theological consequences. The child's death is both judgment and mercy -- it removes the living evidence of the sin.

David's grief gives way not to stoic resignation but to theological hope.

verses.note: HEBREW: The angel (mal'akh) of the LORD at the threshing floor of Araunah — the angel of death is stayed by divine mercy at the very site where the Temple will be built (2 Chr 3:1). Judgment halted becomes the place of atonement.

verses.flow: And when David saw the angel who was striking the people, he said to the LORD, "Behold, it is I who have sinned, and it is I who have done wrong; but these sheep, what have they done? Please let Your hand be against me and against my father's house."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "These sheep, what have they done?" — David intercedes for his people, offering himself in their place. This is the shepherd-king at his most Christlike. CHRIST TYPOLOGY: David offers himself for the guilty flock. Christ, the Good Shepherd, actually accomplished what David could only offer.

verses.flow: However, the king said to Araunah, "No, but I will surely buy it from you for a price; for I will not offer burnt offerings to the LORD my God which cost me nothing." So David bought the threshing floor and the oxen for fifty shekels of silver.
verses.note: HEBREW: lo a'aleh la-YHWH Elohai olot chinnam — "I will not offer to the LORD my God burnt offerings that cost me nothing." This is the principle of costly worship. Sacrifice that costs nothing is not sacrifice. THEOLOGICAL: True devotion requires something of us — our time, treasure, comfort, pride. Cheap grace is no grace.

verses.flow: And David built there an altar to the LORD, and offered burnt offerings and peace offerings. Thus the LORD was moved by prayer for the land, and the plague was held back from Israel.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: David builds the altar, offers sacrifices, and the plague is stayed. The threshing floor of Araunah becomes the site of Solomon's Temple (2 Chr 3:1). The place of judgment becomes the place of mercy — the pattern of the cross. CHRIST TYPOLOGY: The threshing floor where judgment was halted by sacrifice becomes the Temple mount. Where an animal died to stay the plague, the Lamb of God would ultimately be offered to stay the wrath of God against sin.

verses.flow: And Gad came to David that day and said to him, "Go up, raise an altar to the LORD on the threshing floor of Araunah the Jebusite."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: 2 Samuel ends not with triumph but with atonement — an altar, a sacrifice, God relenting. The entire Davidic narrative arc moves from anointing through sin to restoration through sacrifice. This is the gospel in miniature.
